segments.s405-g17: بخندد is a classical 3rd‑person singular present form meaning "laughs"; combined with the habitual particle همی it denotes a continuing or customary action ("keeps laughing"). بلبل is the subject "the nightingale." از هر دوان is a prepositional phrase (از + هر + دوان) meaning "from/out of every motion/direction," where دوان (literally "run/rush") is used figuratively.
segments.s405-g18: چو here = "when." بر گل نشیند uses the preposition بر + noun + present (نشینـد) to mean "when it sits/settles upon the flower" (نشینـد is present, 3rd singular). گشاید is a poetic present tense of "open" (literal root گشاد/گشا) and governs زبان "tongue/speech," so the clause means "it opens its tongue" (i.e. begins to sing/speak).
segments.s405-g19: ندانم is the negative present first‑person "I do not know." که introduces the subordinate content clause (here roughly "whether/that"). عاشق گل is a noun phrase "lover of the rose." آمد‌گر is an archaic fused conditional/optative meaning "if he comes/should come" (گر is the old conditional particle attached to آمد). ابر follows as a nominative/oblique noun here ("cloud"); the punctuation/word-order is compressed in poetic style.
segments.s405-g20: چو = "when." از + ابر = "from/out of the cloud." بینم is 1st‑person present "I see." خروش is a noun "roar/uproar," and هژبر is a proper name or epithet; together the phrase means "I see the roar/clamor of Hojabr." Word order is subject‑verb‑object flexible in poetry, so temporal clause (چو ...) precedes the seeing clause.
segments.s405-g21: بدرد here functions as a jussive/optative idea ("let it tear/have effect"), followed by همی which is the classical continuous/intensifying particle; باد in this context can be read as the agent "wind" but in classical poetry also supports an optative sense ("may the wind ..."). پیراهنش is the object "his shirt" (پیراهن + enclitic ـش "his"). The clause is elliptical and idiomatic: a wish that the wind do something to his shirt.
segments.s405-g22: درفشان is an adjectival/participial form meaning "shining/blazing" (here describing the resulting state). شود is the present/passive auxiliary "becomes/is made" (3rd sing.). آتش اندر تنش is a locative phrase: آتش "fire" + اندر "in/within" + تنش "his body" (تن + enclitic ـش). Together: "fire becomes blazing within his body."
segments.s405-g23: به عشق is an ablative/instrumental phrase "through/because of love." هوا is the subject/noun "air/atmosphere." بر زمین شد is a verbal phrase (بر + زمین + شد) meaning "came upon/was raised over the earth" (شد = became/happened). گوا is an adverbial particle meaning "apparently/seemingly," qualifying the statement that follows.
segments.s405-g24: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning "near/close to." خورشید is the noun "the sun." فرمانروا is a predicative noun "ruler/sovereign." The phrase as a whole states that the air (or what was described) seems to rule/hold sway close to the sun; word order is compact, typical of classical Persian epigrammatic lines.
segments.s405-g25: که here functions as an interrogative/relative 'who' (archaic poetic use). داند is 3rd-person singular present 'knows'; the following که introduces the subordinate clause ('what ...'). بلبل is the subject 'the nightingale'. چه is the interrogative 'what', and گوید would be the verb 'says' but its listed senses don't match the verbal use here (hence flagged below). همی is an archaic continuous/adverbial particle ('keeps/continually') modifying the verb.
segments.s405-g26: به + زیر + گل‌اندر form a locative phrase 'beneath / down among the flowers' with به used to mark location; گل‌اندر is a compound 'among flowers'. چه is again an interrogative 'what'. موید in the text appears to be uncertain/archaic here (the provided senses relate to 'hair'), so its verbal/semantic role is unclear and flagged; همی again marks continuous aspect ('keeps / is continually').
segments.s405-g27: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (watch/gaze) + the imperative particle کن 'do' = 'keep watch'. سحرگاه ('at dawn') is the temporal adverbial that follows the imperative. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / until'), and بشنوی is 2nd-person present-subjunctive/optative 'you may hear' (used after تا for purpose or expectation).
segments.s405-g28: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here 'of/from the'), introducing the source of what is to be heard. بلبل is the source 'the nightingale'. سخن is the noun 'speech/words', while گفتنی is a participial/adjectival form 'to be spoken / spoken' (i.e. 'spoken words'). پهلوی postmodifies the phrase, specifying the language 'Pahlavi' — together: 'the words spoken in Pahlavi'.
segments.s405-g29: همی is the habitual/continuous particle ('keeps on'). نالد is 3rd-person singular present 'he laments/moans'. از introduces the cause/source 'about / because of' and مرگ اسفندیار is the object/cause 'Esfandiyār's death'.
segments.s405-g30: ندارد is the negative present of 'to have' ('he does not have'). به جز is the exception phrase 'except'. ناله is the object 'a lament/cry', ز (short for از) + و = 'from him' (زو = ز + او, archaic/poetic clitic) indicating source, and یادگار is 'keepsake/memento' — word order places the thing possessed after the verb: 'he has nothing except the lament from him as a keepsake.'
segments.s405-g31: چو is a temporal/comparative conjunction here 'when / as'. آواز رستم is the noun phrase 'Rostam's voice' (آواز = voice; رستم = Rostam). شب تیره, ابر are a sequence of nominative/adjunct nouns 'the dark night, [and] the cloud' that set the scene; the order is poetic and descriptive.
segments.s405-g32: بدرد here is used in the sense 'to rend / to cause pain' (transitive effect on feelings). دل و گوش are the two direct objects 'heart and ear' (i.e. it pierces both emotion and hearing). غران‌هژبر is an epithet/name in apposition/description ('roaring Gharrān‑Hezbar'); its role is chiefly descriptive and vocative in tone — the whole phrase describes the violent, roaring effect of the sound.
segments.s405-g1: کنون is an adverb meaning “now/presently” placed at the sentence start for emphasis. خورد باید is a classical Persian construction where خورد (verbal noun/infinitive of “to eat/partake”) precedes the modal باید (“must/ought”), so literal order is “to eat must” → “one must eat/partake.” می‌خوش‌گوار is an adjectival/nominal phrase (mi‑khosh‑gavar) meaning “pleasant/agreeable” describing what should be partaken; the prefixed می‑ here is lexical/poetic, not the modern progressive marker.
segments.s405-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason (“for/because”). می‌بوی is an impersonal/present verb meaning “is smelled” or “one smells”; مشک is the thing smelled (musk) and آید (“comes”) expresses origin/motion, with از + جویبار marking source (“from the stream”). Word order places the sensing verb before the coming verb, a normal poetic sequence: “one smells; musk comes from the stream.”
segments.s405-g3: This is a balanced poetic clause: Noun + پر (+ complement). هوا پر خروش = “the air is full of clamor/roar,” and زمین پر ز جوش = “the earth is full (پر) of surge/bubbling,” with ز the poetic preposition “of/from.” پر functions as an adjective-like predicate meaning “full (of).”
segments.s405-g4: خنک is an adjective meaning “cool/refreshing.” آنک is a classical relative/particle (“that/which”) introducing the following clause; here it links the coolness to its effect. دل، شاد دارد به نوش is a small clause where دل (the heart) is the affected noun, شاد دارد literally “has/keeps (it) glad” — دَارد is the present verb ‘has/causes’ — and به نوش means “by/with drink” (نوش = draught/drink). The relative phrasing thus yields “the coolness which makes the heart glad with drink.”
segments.s405-g5: درم دارد = “he has dirhams” (درم = coin, دارد = possesses). The list continues with و نقل و جام نبید = “and (has) sweets and a cup of wine”; نقل here is the confection/sweet (not ‘report’), and جام نبید = “a cup of wine.” Word order places possessed nouns after the verb دارد in the classical style.
segments.s405-g6: سر گوسفندی (object phrase) precedes the verbal complex; گوسفندی is a genitive/adjective meaning “of a sheep” (i.e., a sheep’s head). تواند is the modal “can/is able” (third person), and برید here functions as the classical subjunctive/infinitival form after تواند meaning “to cut/slaughter” (classical Persian often uses the past stem in such constructions). So the clause reads “he can cut off a sheep’s head.”
segments.s405-g7: مرا نیست is a fixed classical expression meaning “I do not have it” (مرا = to/for me as the possessor, followed by نیست “is not”). The second half, فرخ مر آن را که هست, is an exclamatory/optative clause: فرخ (= “fortunate/blessed”) is predicated of the (implied) person, and آن را که هست is a relative clause “who has that.” In short: “I don’t have it; fortunate is he who has it.” (Note: the segment uses archaic word order and particles typical of Ferdowsi.)
segments.s405-g8: ببخشای is an optative/jussive form meaning “may he give/grant” (a wish or hortative directed at the person who has it); the direct object is the previously mentioned آن را. بر مردم تنگدست uses بر + people to indicate the beneficiary/recipient group “to the poor people.” The whole line is a wish: “may he grant it to the poor.”
segments.s405-g9: همه بوستان (subject) = a quantified NP 'all the gardens' with همه as a universal determiner preceding the noun. زیر برگ گل is a locative/predicative phrase 'under the leaf (petal) of the flower' (برگ گل = 'petal'); ست is the poetic/fused copula (گلست = گل + است) marking the predicate. Word order is subject + predicate (typical classical Persian).
segments.s405-g10: همه کوه = 'every/ all the mountain(s)' (همه again quantifies). پرلاله is an adjectival compound (پر- + لاله) meaning 'full of tulips' or 'tulip‑covered'; و links the two adjectival predicates; سنبلست is 'سنبل + است' (adjectival predicate 'is hyacinth‑filled' / 'hyacinth‑like') so the whole clause reads 'every mountain is full of tulips and hyacinths.' The copula is again fused in the final word.
segments.s405-g11: به پالیز is a locative/dir. phrase ('to/into the orchard' or idiomatically 'in the orchard'); بلبل is the subject 'the nightingale'. بنالد is a present/habitual form formed with the prefix بـ + root نال (classical present tense) meaning 'laments/keeps lamenting'; همی adds durative emphasis ('still/continually').
segments.s405-g12: گل is the subject 'the flower.' از introduces the cause/source ('from' / 'because of') and links ناله او ('his lament') as the reason. ببالد is another present/habitual form (بـ + root بال) here meaning something like 'swells/comes to pride' or 'takes pride' in a poetic sense; همی again marks continuous action. Word order is subject — prepositional/cause phrase — verb.
segments.s405-g13: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when.' از ابر is a source phrase 'from the cloud.' بینم is first‑person singular present ('I see' / 'I behold'); همی gives an ongoing sense ('I keep seeing' or 'I continually behold'). باد و نم are the things perceived ('wind and dew'). Word order shows a classical flexible placement of adverbials and verb.
segments.s405-g14: ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know' (ن + دانم). که introduces an indirect question/complement clause here ('that/why'); نرگس is the topic/subject of the subordinate clause ('the narcissus' — the flower); چرا شد is 'why has ... become' (چرا = 'why', شد = past/ perfective 'became'); دژم is a predicative adjective 'sullen/grim.' The clause thus reads 'I do not know why the narcissus became sullen.'
segments.s405-g15: شب تیره = 'the dark night' (adnominal phrase). بلبل is the subject. نخسپد is a negative present form (نـ + خسبـ) meaning 'does not sleep' in classical usage; it is a present/habitual negated verb, and همی adds durative emphasis ('keeps not sleeping' / 'cannot sleep').
segments.s405-g16: گل is the subject. از باد و باران marks the cause/agent ('from/by wind and rain'). بجنبد is the present/habitual form with prefix بـ + جنب (here 'to move/stir, to tremble'); همی again gives the continuous sense. The clause means the flower keeps being stirred/trembled by wind and rain.

sentences.s405-s9: The nightingale keeps laughing from every motion; when it alights on the rose it opens its tongue.
sentences.s405-s10: I do not know whether a lover of the rose has come; when I look from the cloud I see the roar of Hojabr.
sentences.s405-s11: May the wind rend his shirt; fire becomes blazing within his body.
sentences.s405-s12: Through love the air came upon the earth; it seems to have become ruler close to the sun.
sentences.s405-s13: Who knows what the nightingale keeps saying? What is he doing down among the flowers?
sentences.s405-s14: Keep watch at dawn until you hear the nightingale's speech spoken in Pahlavi.
sentences.s405-s15: He keeps lamenting Esfandiyār's death; he has nothing except the lament from him as a keepsake.
sentences.s405-s16: When Rostam's voice sounds, the dark night and the clouds rend heart and ear—roaring (like) Gharrān-Hezbar.
sentences.s405-s1: Now one ought to partake of the pleasant (treat), for the scent of musk comes from the stream.
sentences.s405-s2: The air is full of clamor and the earth full of bubbling; that coolness which makes the heart glad with drink.
sentences.s405-s3: He has dirhams, sweets and a cup of wine; he can cut off a sheep's head.
sentences.s405-s4: I do not have it; fortunate is he who has it — may he grant it to the poor people.
sentences.s405-s5: All the gardens lie beneath a flower's petal; every mountain is full of tulips and hyacinths.
sentences.s405-s6: In the orchard the nightingale keeps lamenting; the flower, from his moan, keeps swelling (or taking pride).
sentences.s405-s7: When from the cloud I behold wind and dew, I do not know why the narcissus has become sullen.
sentences.s405-s8: On the dark night the nightingale does not sleep; the flower, from wind and rain, keeps trembling.
